title: Amma's Words; Only “Amma” Can Shed Light on Them!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source pages confirm the disputed date as 9 November 2013 and preserve two intentional English quotations concerning Katchatheevu. An OCR-only ornament fragment preceding the salutation is excluded from the derived reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: It is twenty days today since Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa began administering the government from Kodanad. How many more days, who knows? We may expect her to return to the capital at least to participate in the Republic Day celebrations on 26 January. Reports say that the Governor's Address is on the 23rd. Perhaps, just as they shifted the Legislative Assembly from its new premises in the Omandurar complex back to the old building, they may shift even the Governor's Address to Kodanad!

In these circumstances, a statement has been issued in the Chief Minister's name, described as though it came from Chennai. She announces that Indian and Sri Lankan fishermen will meet for talks on the 20th and that all 275 Tamil Nadu fishermen languishing in Sri Lankan prisons will be released within a day or two.

At my request, DMK Parliamentary Party leader T.R. Baalu arranged for representatives of Tamil Nadu's fishermen to meet the Prime Minister in Delhi on 27 December 2013. At that very meeting, the Prime Minister assured both the representatives and T.R. Baalu that delegates of the two countries' fishermen would meet around 20 January. Every newspaper carried the news at the time. Fifteen days later, the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister has repeated the same news in her statement. Perhaps, because she was at Kodanad, she did not read what the Prime Minister had announced.

However it happens, the talks must take place, good decisions must be reached, and the fishermen's problems must find a solution. Before the talks, however, the Sri Lankan government must release every Indian fisherman it has arrested, kept in extended custody and imprisoned there. That would greatly assist the parties in reaching an amicable outcome. In today's statement, the Chief Minister says with certainty on behalf of the Tamil Nadu government that they will be released. I do not know how Jayalalithaa received this information. Did it come from the Central government, or directly from Sri Lankan President Rajapaksa?

In her statement, Jayalalithaa lists the assistance the AIADMK government has given fishermen. If such assistance is to be recalled, I could go on listing what the DMK government did between May 2006 and January 2011: Rs. 88.5729 crore in relief; an increase in relief payments from Rs. 1,200 to Rs. 1,800; Rs. 61.57 crore in relief for fisherwomen; Rs. 14.33 crore during the fishing-ban period; an increase in compensation to the family of a fisherman who dies to Rs. 3 lakh; Rs. 21.12 crore sanctioned under the Ma. Singaravelar Memorial Housing Scheme; fishing harbours at Colachel and Thengapattinam costing Rs. 27 crore; a fishing harbour at Poompuhar in Nagapattinam district costing Rs. 50 crore; and Rs. 4.52 crore in assistance through the Tamil Nadu Fishermen Welfare Board that we created.

But, as usual, the Chief Minister's statement also makes many unnecessary attacks upon me. Referring to her appeal to the Indian Prime Minister by letter on 7 January 2014, I asked what answer she had to the Prime Minister's statement that the Central government had written asking Tamil Nadu to specify a date for a meeting between fishermen's representatives of the two countries but had received no reply. She responded that the proverb about “fishing in troubled waters” came to her mind. The Chief Minister must understand that this is not a problem of fishing in a muddied pond, but of fishing in the middle of the sea.

I am not fishing in troubled waters. Why did she not answer the report broadcast by *Headlines Today* on 7 January under the headline, “Jayalalithaa is using the Tamil Nadu fishermen's issue to secure votes”? Is that television channel run by the DMK? Is it not an ordinary private broadcaster? I merely cited its accusation. Was it not the broadcaster that actually made the charge? Should not the Chief Minister answer it? Instead, Jayalalithaa says in her statement that I am somehow pleading the Central government's case. I did no such thing. I only demonstrated that the Tamil Nadu government caused the delay.

The Chief Minister has issued a statement as though she had accomplished something extraordinary on the fishermen's issue. Who delayed the meeting of the two countries' representatives? Is that not what the television channel asked? It pointed out: “Jayalalithaa continues to blame the Central government, but at the same time she herself is delaying a solution. Since 16 March 2013, Prime Minister Dr Manmohan Singh has written eleven letters to Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa about the fishermen's issue. In most of them, he urged her to cooperate in arranging a meeting at which Indian and Sri Lankan fishermen's representatives could hold talks.” Is this true or not? If it is, who is to blame for so much delay? Who is fishing in troubled waters? Is Jayalalithaa prepared to answer?

The Prime Minister wrote as early as 16 March 2013. Why was there no reply? Six months later, only on 20 September, did the Chief Minister write conveying the Tamil Nadu government's consent to the meeting. The Prime Minister himself thanked her for it in his letter dated 9 November 2013. If Jayalalithaa genuinely cared about Tamil Nadu fishermen's suffering, would she have delayed her reply for six months? Whenever fishermen are detained, does writing one letter to the Prime Minister and hurriedly publicising it in the newspapers amount to protecting Indian fishermen?

This is precisely what I pointed out when I asked about the *Headlines Today* report. Unable to answer, the Chief Minister has, as usual, chosen to accuse me. Jayalalithaa says that I adopt one position in office and another outside it, playing a double role.

After passing an Assembly resolution on 16 April 2002 demanding that Liberation Tigers leader Prabhakaran be arrested and brought to India, she now passes Assembly resolutions supporting Sri Lankan Tamils. That is what a double position means. Yet such a person says in yesterday's statement that I change my position frequently like a “chameleon.”

When innocent Tamils were massacred in Sri Lanka, did this same Jayalalithaa not proclaim, “In war, it is natural for innocent people to be killed”? Was that not a statement supporting the Sinhala government? Who is performing the double act? Who is playing the chameleon? Let the people of Tamil Nadu understand.

Whenever Jayalalithaa issues a statement attacking me, she accuses me of having handed Katchatheevu away. Although I have answered many times, she will not let it go. In a letter dated 30 September 1994 to Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao, Jayalalithaa, then Chief Minister, wrote: “The ceding of this tiny island to the Island Nation had been done by the Government of India in the interest of better bilateral relations.” In other words, she herself wrote that the Indian government ceded the tiny island of Katchatheevu to Sri Lanka for better relations between the two countries. Why does she now leap upon me? Is politics the reason?

That is not all. In a letter to Prime Minister Vajpayee dated 23 July 2003, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa described what she called the only way to preserve cordial relations between India and Sri Lanka while protecting the traditional rights of Tamil Nadu fishermen. She wrote: “The best possible solution is to get the island of Katcha Theevu and adjacent seas on lease in perpetuity solely for fishing, drying of nets and pilgrimage. Sri Lanka’s Sovereignty over Katcha Theevu could be upheld at the same time.”

Thus Jayalalithaa wrote that Katchatheevu and the neighbouring seas could be obtained on permanent lease for Tamil Nadu fishermen to fish, dry their nets and make pilgrimages, while Sri Lanka's sovereignty over Katchatheevu could simultaneously be upheld. Yet it is this same Jayalalithaa who says I surrendered the island. Writing that letter to the Prime Minister and accusing me now is the double role.

Speaking in the Assembly on 20 April 1992, she said: “We adopted a resolution here seeking the recovery of Katchatheevu and sent it to the Central government. But recovering Katchatheevu does not appear to be something that can happen quickly.” Concealing this completely, she says in yesterday's statement that she will certainly recover it. Whom is she trying to deceive? Who is performing the double act?

The Jayalalithaa who passed Assembly resolutions saying India should not attend the Commonwealth conference in Sri Lanka and that economic sanctions should be imposed upon Sri Lanka was also the one who demolished the wall of the Mullivaikkal Memorial in Thanjavur, built in memory of the Liberation Tigers' war. That is Jayalalithaa's deceitful drama and chameleon conduct.

Jayalalithaa is now the Chief Minister. I hope that at least hereafter she will abandon words such as deceitful drama, double role and chameleon, issue statements with civility, and criticise the opposition in a civil manner. Otherwise, the words used in “Amma's” statement will fit “Amma” herself.
Affectionately,
M.K.
13 January 2014
